public:: true
title:: Andrea Palladio

# Andrea Palladio

Andrea Palladio (1508-1580) was a Republic-of-Venice architect whose
treatise [[I Quattro Libri dell'Architettura]] codified the syntax of
[[Classical Orders]] for the European tradition. His built work — most
famously [[Villa Rotonda]] and the [[Basilica Palladiana]] — propagated
the idea that ideal proportion is a discoverable property of geometry,
not a stylistic preference.

Palladio worked across the Veneto: villas at [[Villa Foscari]],
[[Villa Barbaro]], and churches in Venice including [[San Giorgio Maggiore]]
and [[Il Redentore]].

His influence on later architecture was disproportionate to his lifetime
recognition. The Anglo-American tradition he seeded is known as
[[Palladianism]].
